pages.text: Mechanical properties of expanded polystyrene lightweight aggregate concrete and brick Yi Xu ⇑, Linhua Jiang, Jinxia Xu, Yang Li College of Mechanics and Materials, Hohai University, Nanjing 210098, China article info Article history: Received 18 March 2011 Received in revised form 7 August 2011 Accepted 7 August 2011 Available online 28 September 2011 Keywords: Expanded polystyrene Lightweight concrete Mechanical properties Brick Masonry abstract In this study, mix proportion parameters of expanded polystyrene (EPS) lightweight aggregate concrete are analyzed by using Taguchi’s approach. The density, compressive strength and stress–strain behavior were tested. The optimal mixture of EPS lightweight aggregate concrete was selected among experiments under consideration to manufacture the lightweight hollow bricks. The results show that EPS dosage has the most signiﬁcant effect on compressive strength of EPS lightweight aggregate concrete, then water and cement ratio, while the content of cement and sand ratio play a comparatively less important part. The relationship between density and compressive strength of EPS lightweight aggregate concrete is proposed as f c = 2.43 /C2 c2.997 /C2 10/C0 9. The legitimacy of the use of EPS lightweight bricks made by EPS lightweight aggregate concrete is conﬁrmed. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Recently, with the rapid development of high rise buildings, ﬂoating marine platforms, larger-sized and long-span concrete structures, lightweight concrete (LWC) has been a promising mod- ern construction material [1–4]. In comparison with ordinary con- crete, LWC shows some excellent characteristics such as lower density, higher speciﬁc strength, better thermal insulation and greater energy absorption which can be obtained by replacing stan- dard aggregate totally or partially by lightweight aggregate (LWA). Lightweight aggregate are broadly classiﬁed into two types: nat- ural (pumice, diatomite, volcanic cinders, etc.) and artiﬁcial (perlite, clay, sintered ﬂy ash, expanded shale, etc.). Expanded polystyrene (EPS) is a type of artiﬁcial lightweight aggregate with the density of only 10–30 kg/m 3 [5]. Due to the ultra-lightweight density of EPS, the lightweight aggregate concrete is prone to segregation dur- ing casting. Many researchers have studied to overcome this draw- back. Babu and Babu [6] added ultra ﬁne silica fume to improve the bonding between EPS beads and cement paste. Roy et al. [7] utilized superplasticizer to avoid the segregation of EPS spheres. Chen and Liu [8] applied styrene-butadiene rubber (SBR) latex to improve the strength of concrete. Here, few special investigations of the EPS lightweight bricks made by EPS lightweight aggregate concrete and the mechanical properties of EPS brick masonry have been reported [9]. The brick is produced through extrusion molding technique which can avoid the segregation of EPS beads. The production of EPS lightweight concrete bricks with low thermal conductivity can also provide an alternative solution for the utilization and development of EPS lightweight aggregate concrete in engineering considering the economic and practical factors [10]. In this paper, the main objective was to provide some basic information about the relationship between the elementary com- position and the properties of EPS concrete by using the Taguchi method which was a statistical and systematic approach. The den- sity, compressive strength and stress

have on the mechanical properties and thermal performance of EPS lightweight concrete. In general, the coefﬁcient of thermal conductivity is closely related with the density. The lower the density of concrete, the higher is its thermal insulation. But there is an inverse relation that low density results in a decrease in compressive strength. In the present study, the best possible levels of mix proportions are inves- tigated for the maximization of compressive strength and for the minimization of density. Taguchi method was introduced as a useful engineering methodology to ﬁnd proper combination of structural parameters and to analyze with minimum number of experiments [12]. Therefore nine experiments were carried out by using an L9 orthogonal array. The factors and levels to be considered for concrete are gi- ven in Table 1 . The EPS concretes were initially designed as per Chinese speciﬁca- tion JGJ 51-2002 and modiﬁed by incorporating the EPS beads at different volume percentages in the concretes. 2.3. Experimental procedure Materials were mixed in the following order: Firstly, EPS beads were wetted ini- tially with a part of the mixing water. Secondly, cement, sand and the remaining water were added and mixed for about 3 min at low speed. Then, crushed stone was added and mixed for another 3–5 min until a uniform concrete had been ob- tained. This sequence has been recommended by the pertinent study reported by Babu and Babu [6], and Chen and Liu [13]. The concrete specimens were cast in steel molds, followed immediately by cur- ing at room temperature for 24 h before being demolded. After demolding, the specimens were stored in a control room maintained at 20 ± 1 /C176C and 95% RH until the days of test. The compressive strength and density of EPS lightweight concretes were mea- sured on cube specimens (100 mm /C2 100 mm /C2 100 mm) at the age of 7 days and 28 days, at a loading rate of 2.5 kN/s. Cylindrical samples of 150 mm diameter and 300 mm height were cast on which the study of stress–strain behavior were carried out under axial compression at the 28 day aged. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Workability and density Most of these EPS lightweight concretes displayed good work- ability. However, Nos. 3, 5, and 7 mixtures showed the tendency of segregation and collapse due to their higher EPS aggregate content (25%), resulting in a lower degree of compaction in the test specimens, which partly contributed to the reduction in strength. It was observed that when the EPS ratio of the concrete was in- creased, the workability was reduced because of the hydrophobic property and the increased surface area of EPS beads. Density is one of the important parameters, which can control many physical properties in lightweight concrete and it is mainly controlled by the amount and density of lightweight aggregate [14]. In general, the density of EPS concrete decreases with an in- crease in thermal insulation property, but results in a decrease in compressive strength of the concrete. A comprehensive summary of Taguchi orthogonal array design for EPS lightweight concretes and the results of density and compressive strength are presented in Table 2 . The density of the EPS lightweight concrete was shown to vary from about 1700 kg/m 3 to 2100 kg/m 3. There is a saving in dead- load between 10% and 30%, if the density of conventional normal weight concrete is considered as 2400 kg/m 3. The density mean values of each factor in corresponding with l

the sand ratio (%). Correlation coefﬁcient ( R) of these proposed relations is 0.986, indicating strong correlation. 3.2. Compressive strength It is to be observed from Table 2 , the percentage increase of strength at 7 days is about 75% of 28 days strength. In contrast, Cook reported that EPS concretes developed almost 95% of the 28 day strength in 7 days, and Kan reported that the concretes developed more than 80% of the 28 day strength in 7 days, com- pared to the 70% for normal weight concrete [9,14]. The main effect plot graphs of factors for compressive strength of EPS lightweight concrete are shown in Figs. 3 and 4. They are plotted by considering means of responses at each level of the parameters, as outlined in Table 4 . It can be found that volume of EPS had the most signiﬁcant effect on both the 7 days strength and the 28 days strength, then water and cement ratio. The content of cement and sand ratio had lesser effects compared to other factors. In this research the minimum and maximum compressive strength at the age of 7 days were 7.31 and 15.22 MPa, and the val- ues at the age of 28 days are 7.85 and 20.77 MPa, respectively (see Table 2 ). According to Figs. 3 and 4, increasing of EPS volume and W/C parameters decreases the compressive strength, while increasing sand ratio parameter value increases the compressive strength of EPS lightweight concrete. When the content of cement is increased in the concrete mix, the compressive strength ﬁrst falls and then rises. The mechanical behavior of the concrete specimens containing EPS aggregates is quite different from that of the normal concrete. The failure mode of EPS concrete under compression exhibits more gradual instead of the typical brittle failure. Compared to the ef- fects of EPS dosage and water–cement ratio, the effect of cement content is negligible. Since EPS aggregates have almost zero strength, the strength of EPS concrete is mostly inﬂuenced by the EPS aggregate dosage. Therefore, even after adding 500 kg/m 3 of cement, strength increase is very less. The optimal mix-design for maximization of compressive strength was 400 kg/m 3 cement, 0.45 W/C, 15% volume of EPS, 41% sand ratio. Through linear regression analysis, the empirical relations ob- tained can be written as Eqs. (2) and (3) : f7 ¼ 34:911 /C0 0:006x1 /C0 37:700x2 /C0 42:667x3 þ 16:444x4 ð2Þ f28 ¼ 40:029 /C0 0:006x1 /C0 40:900x2 /C0 74:733x3 þ 33:556x4 ð3Þ where f7, f28 represent compressive strength (MPa) at 7 days and 28 days, respectively. Correlation coefﬁcients ( R) of these proposed relations are 0.975 and 0.957, indicating signiﬁcant correlations. 3.3. Compressive strength vs. density The variation of compressive strength with density of EPS light- weight concrete is represented in Fig. 5. As can be noted, the com- pressive strength is decreasing as the density decreases with an increase in EPS percentage. This fact is similar to the previous works. Roy et al. [7] developed an empirical model suitable to very lightweight concrete and the result showed a power law in strength evolution. Kan and Demirbog˘ a [14] investigated the rela- tionship between hardened concrete density and compressive strength of lightweight concrete with a density of about 980– 2025 kg/m3 (fc = 13.8 Ln (c) /C0 85). Babu and Babu [9] studied the lightweight expanded polystyrene concrete containing silica fume with the density varying from 1500 to 2000 kg/m 3 and the strength appears to increase linearly with an increase in co

3.4. Stress–strain behavior Fig. 6 shows the stress–strain behavior for all nine samples of EPS lightweight concrete in compression. It can be found that the shape of all the diagrams was similar to the normal weight con- crete. It constituted an linear range at the onset, followed by a non- linear response of stress increment for gain in strain, up to a maximum stress. After that the stress decreased inelastically, while the strain enhanced. With increased in the compressive strength (i.e., the percentage volume of EPS decreased) the length of the elastic portion of the stress–strain curve get bigger and the slope increased. Also, the concrete failed at lower strain levels as the strength level decreased. This is not in accordance with the previ- ous research ﬁndings that as the strength level increases the concretes fail at lower strain levels [9], but similar diagram is ob- tained in literature [17–19]. The probable reason for this difference may be that a naphthalene-based superplasticizer and an air- entraining admixture were used in Babu’s experiment. As the amount of EPS sphere added into the concrete increased, the spec- imens remaining good interfacial bonding performance between EPS particles and cement paste. While no bonding additives or admixtures were added in our present study. 4. Application The major purpose of this paper is to ﬁnd out the optimum mix ratio of EPS lightweight concrete to mass produce the hollow bricks. From an economic and energy-saving conservation point of view, the EPS lightweight hollow bricks have obvious beneﬁts of improving thermal insulation, absorbing sound characteristics as well as reducing the loads of building walls. There is a contradiction in the comparison of optimum mix pro- portions of maximization of compressive strength and minimiza- tion of density (i.e., low thermal conductivity). Due to the cost- effective concern and other practical consideration, a compromise composition based on the preliminary trial experiments was ap- plied for utilization. The proposed lightweight bricks with the unit dimension of 190 mm /C2 240 mm /C2 90 mm and the shell thickness of 30 mm were molded in a compacted machine, de-molded immediately and transferred to a storage area for curing up to 28 days in normal air condition. The model of unit is represented in Fig. 7 . In order to analyze the mechanical behavior of EPS lightweight hollow brick masonry, three specimens of size 240 mm /C2 390 mm /C2 720 mm and six specimens of size 390 mm /C2 Table 4 Mean value of each factor in correspondence with levels – compressive strength. Xu et al. / Construction and Building Materials 27 (2012) 32–38 35

horizontal deformation developed without relevant increasing of the compression stress and the masonry fractured with vertical cracks. In the test of EPS brick masonry, Ncr/Nu was about 40– 55%. It was similar to the normal brick masonry. The elasticity modulus has been considered as the secant modulus at 40% of the ultimate stress. The average value of the three test series was equal to 6900 MPa. The shear strength results of six masonry specimens were 0.15, 0.09, 0.17, 0.16, 0.14 and 0.18 MPa, respectively. They would basi- cally satisfy the requirements of the speciﬁcation GB50003-2001. The failure mode is presented in Figs. 11 and 12. The mortar cores had been formed by the ﬁlling of the brick cavities with fresh mor- tar, which could contribute to a resistance in shear of the masonry work [20]. But it took place not in a compact and uniform manner. Among six shear strength values reported here, 0.09 MPa is least. It would probably manifest the ﬁssuring at the brick/mortar interface of the second masonry specimen before the load was applied. 5. Conclusions The following conclusions can be drawn based on the results of this study: (1) EPS dosage played the most important role in determining the density and compressive strength of LWA concrete, then water and cement ratio. The density value of EPS concrete showed an almost linear decrease as the volume of EPS and W/C parameters increased, but results in a decrease in compressive strength. The relationship between density and compressive strength was proposed as f c = 2.43 /C2 c2.997 /C2 10/C0 9. (2) The shape of the stress–strain diagram of EPS concrete was similar to the normal weight concrete. With increased in the compressive strength, the length of the elastic portion of the stress–strain curve get bigger and the slope increased. The concrete failed at lower strain levels as the strength level decreased. (3) From the economic and practical concern, a compromise composition of EPS concrete was chosen to produce EPS lightweight hollow bricks. The compressive strength and shear strength values of EPS lightweight hollow brick masonry could satisfy the mechanical requirements of the speciﬁcation GB50003-2001. 290 mm /C2 240 mm were prepared and tested for compressive strength and shear strength respectively, according to Chinese Standard GBJ129-90. The tests were executed in conditions of applying the load at a uniform rate so that the maximum load was reached in 1–3 min. The masonry prisms were considered as extracted from a real masonry wall. The arrangements are shown in Fig. 8 . The compression test results are listed in Table 5. From analysis, the testing value of compressive strength is slightly higher than the calculated value using the formula prescribed in Chinese speciﬁca- tion GB50003-2001. It can be considered that the EPS lightweight bricks were suitable for masonry walls. The cracking mode is illus- trated in Fig. 9 and it appeared that the cracks along the head joints and crossed also the joined bricks. The behavior of masonry prisms in compression is plotted in Fig. 10 . It was similar to a concrete specimen stress–strain curve. When the applied load was less than the load at the initial cracking of the masonry, the stress–strain law was quasi-elastic. Then, Fig. 8.
